categories.label: Missing Tests
categories.description: Implementation submitted without adequate test coverage for the specified acceptance criteria or changed code paths.
signals: No test file created or modified alongside implementation
Acceptance criteria not covered by assertions
Edge cases described in the scenario have no corresponding test
categories.guidance: Every acceptance criterion must have a corresponding test assertion. Create or update test files alongside implementation files. Verify each Given/When/Then clause is exercised by at least one test case.

categories.label: Wrong Pattern
categories.description: Implementation uses a pattern that conflicts with established project conventions, architecture decisions, or idiomatic Go style.
signals: Using shared memory where channels are the project convention
Bypassing dependency injection with package-level singletons
Ignoring established error wrapping conventions
Using deprecated or non-idiomatic constructs
categories.guidance: Review the relevant ADRs and CLAUDE.md conventions before implementing. When in doubt, follow the pattern used in the nearest existing file. Prefer channels over shared memory for concurrency. Wrap errors with context using fmt.Errorf("operation: %w", err).

categories.label: SOP Violation
categories.description: Implementation violates a Standard Operating Procedure injected into the task context.
signals: SOP rule explicitly referenced in review feedback
Required checklist item skipped
Mandatory validation or guard clause omitted
categories.guidance: Re-read each SOP rule in the task context carefully. SOPs are non-negotiable constraints, not suggestions. If a SOP conflicts with the implementation approach, raise a question rather than ignoring the rule.

categories.label: Incomplete Implementation
categories.description: Implementation is missing one or more components required to satisfy the acceptance criteria.
signals: TODO or placeholder left in submitted code
Acceptance criterion has no corresponding code path
Function stub with no body beyond a panic or empty return
Required integration point not wired up
categories.guidance: All acceptance criteria must be fully addressed before marking the task complete. Remove TODOs and stubs. Trace each criterion through the code to verify the full path is implemented and reachable.

categories.label: Edge Case Missed
categories.description: Implementation does not handle boundary conditions or exceptional inputs specified in the scenario or implied by the domain.
signals: No nil or zero-value guard where the type allows it
Empty collection not handled separately from non-empty
Error from external call silently dropped
Boundary value (max, min, empty string) not tested
categories.guidance: For every input parameter, ask: what happens when it is nil, empty, zero, or at its maximum value? For every external call, ask: what happens when it returns an error? Write a test for each answer that differs from the happy path.

categories.label: API Contract Mismatch
categories.description: Implementation diverges from the API contract specified by the architect or defined in the task's acceptance criteria.
signals: Function signature differs from the contract (wrong types, extra or missing parameters)
JSON field names or types do not match the OpenAPI spec
HTTP status codes differ from the specified contract
Interface method not fully implemented
categories.guidance: Cross-reference the implementation against the API contract in the task context. Signatures, field names, types, and status codes must match exactly. When the contract is ambiguous, ask a clarifying question rather than guessing.

categories.label: Scope Violation
categories.description: Implementation modifies files or introduces changes outside the boundaries defined in the task scope.
signals: Files modified that are listed in the plan's do_not_touch list
Changes made outside the task's declared file scope
Unrelated refactoring bundled with the feature change
Breaking change to a public API not covered by the task
categories.guidance: Only modify files explicitly listed in the task scope or that are direct dependencies of the implementation. Do not refactor unrelated code. If a necessary change is outside scope, raise it as a new task or change proposal rather than including it silently.

categories.label: Secret Exposure
categories.description: Source code contains hardcoded credentials, API keys, tokens, or connection strings that should be externalized.
signals: Hardcoded API key in source code
Hardcoded password or credential
Plaintext token or secret in source
Connection string with embedded credentials
Secret not externalized to environment variable or config
categories.guidance: Never embed secrets in source code. Use environment variables, config files excluded from version control, or a secrets manager. If a value looks like a credential, treat it as one.

categories.label: Input Validation Missing
categories.description: User-controlled input reaches business logic or data layer without validation or sanitization.
signals: No input validation on user-provided data
Unsanitized input passed to business logic
User input concatenated into SQL query
User input used in shell command without sanitization
Path traversal possible via user-controlled file path
Missing validation on HTTP request parameters or body
Cross-site scripting vulnerability from unescaped output
categories.guidance: Validate and sanitize all external input at the trust boundary. Use allowlists over denylists. For SQL, use parameterized queries. For file paths, resolve and check against an allowed base directory. For shell commands, avoid user input entirely or use strict allowlists.

categories.label: Unsafe Error Exposure
categories.description: Internal error details, stack traces, or implementation internals leaked to external consumers via API responses or user-facing output.
signals: Stack trace exposed in API response
Internal error message leaked to client
Implementation details visible in error response
File paths or module names in user-facing errors
Database error details returned to caller
categories.guidance: Return generic error messages to external consumers. Log detailed errors server-side with correlation IDs. Never expose stack traces, file paths, internal component names, or database error details in API responses.
